title: The Missing Generator
brief: At a block of flats in Surulere, Lagos, the 25-KVA backup generator vanished from the locked compound store overnight. The store padlock was opened with a key, not broken. Only three people held keys, and the gate watchman swears nobody drove in.

suspects.name: Tunde Bakare
suspects.motive: Underpaid; owes a cooperative loan due that week
suspects.alibi: Says he was at the gate all night and saw nothing
suspects.note: Sweats and repeats himself when asked about the gate.

suspects.name: Chidinma Okeke
suspects.motive: Controls the store keys and the maintenance budget
suspects.alibi: Says she left at 6pm and was home in Yaba; husband confirms
suspects.note: Calm, produces a tidy logbook unprompted.

suspects.name: Emeka Nwosu
suspects.motive: Knew the gen's resale value; recently lost his workshop lease
suspects.alibi: Says he was home in Ojuelegba; no one to confirm
suspects.note: Overly eager to blame the watchman.

suspects.name: Sola Adebayo
suspects.motive: Could claim insurance on the unit
suspects.alibi: Was in Abuja for a wedding; flight and hotel receipts exist
suspects.note: Furious, demands the police 'do something'.

header.label: Entry method
header.value: Opened with a key, no force

findings.heading: Fresh oil trail
findings.detail: A line of fresh engine-oil drips runs from the store door, past the gate, to the kerb where a vehicle stood. The trail is heavy and continuous, indicating the gen leaked while being wheeled out slowly.

findings.heading: Gate logbook gap
findings.detail: The watchman's vehicle log shows no entries between 10:40pm and 5:00am, though earlier and later pages are filled.

findings.heading: Spare-key prints
findings.detail: Only one set of fingerprints on the store's spare key. The caretaker's master key was found untouched in her handover envelope.

findings.heading: Scrap-yard inflow
findings.detail: On 14 Mar at 7:12am, ₦780,000 was paid into Nwosu's account by 'Ajegunle Metals & Spares', a known generator-and-scrap buyer. Reference line reads 'gen 25k'.

findings.heading: Loan repayment
findings.detail: The watchman repaid his ₦40,000 cooperative loan on 13 Mar — a day BEFORE the theft, from his salary credit. Timing rules out using stolen-gen proceeds.

witnesses.name: Iya Risi
witnesses.relation: Roadside food seller opposite the gate
witnesses.statement: "A small green pickup was parked outside around two in the morning. Two men loaded something heavy and the technician boy who services the gen was directing them."

witnesses.name: Baba Lekan
witnesses.statement: "It is the watchman, I tell you. He always sleeps. He must have sold it."

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Where were you between midnight and 3am on the 14th?

lines.speaker: Emeka Nwosu
lines.text: Sleeping at home in Ojuelegba. Alone.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Do you have a key to the generator store?

lines.speaker: Emeka Nwosu
lines.text: The caretaker gives me the spare when I service it. I always return it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Ajegunle Metals paid you ₦780,000 yesterday morning. For what?

lines.speaker: Emeka Nwosu
lines.text: That... that was an old debt. Old parts I sold them months back.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Their reference says 'gen 25k', dated the morning after a 25-KVA gen went missing.

lines.speaker: Emeka Nwosu
lines.text: I want to call my brother.

timeline.event: Caretaker locks store, hands keys to handover envelope, leaves for Yaba
Last gate log entry before the gap
Green pickup loads heavy item; technician seen directing
Fresh oil trail laid from store to kerb
₦780,000 'gen 25k' paid to Nwosu by Ajegunle Metals

tools.name: Identity Lookup
tools.tagline: Match prints to a registered identity

results.query: Spare store key — single print set
results.note: Only Nwosu's prints are on the spare key that opened the store.

tools.name: Call Log
tools.tagline: Pull recent calls for a number

rows.label: 2:05am
rows.value: Outgoing to unknown — 4 min, near gate

results.note: Calls place Nwosu coordinating a buyer at the time of the theft, breaking his alibi.
No prior record, no link to scrap buyers — the obvious suspect is a dead end.

explanation: Emeka Nwosu, the servicing contractor, stole the generator. He had legitimate spare-key access, so the store was opened with a key and not forced (r1), and his are the only prints on that spare key. The fresh oil trail shows the gen was wheeled out slowly to a waiting vehicle, and Iya Risi reliably saw the technician directing a green pickup at 2am. His call log breaks his 'home asleep' alibi by placing him coordinating a scrap buyer near the gate. The clinching proof is r2: Ajegunle Metals paid him ₦780,000 the next morning with the reference 'gen 25k'. The herrings: the gate logbook gap and Baba Lekan point at the watchman Tunde, but his crime-db lookup is a dead end and he repaid his loan from salary a day BEFORE the theft. The caretaker and landlord both have confirmed alibis and her master key was untouched.

title: The Spoiled Pepper Soup
brief: Eight guests at a small Ibadan birthday party fell ill after eating from one pot of pepper soup; all recovered in hospital. Lab work found a livestock pesticide in that pot only. The pot sat in the kitchen all afternoon, where several people came and went.

suspects.name: Folake Ajayi
suspects.role: Host's sister, cooked the pepper soup
suspects.motive: None obvious; her dish is the one that was tampered with
suspects.alibi: Was serving guests in the parlour from 4pm
suspects.note: Distraught that her cooking is blamed.

suspects.name: Wale Ogunleye
suspects.role: Rival caterer hired for the small chops
suspects.motive: Lost the main-dish contract to Folake; public grudge
suspects.alibi: Says he stayed in the back kitchen prepping all afternoon
suspects.note: Loudly insists he 'only touched the small chops'.

suspects.name: Bisi Adeyemi
suspects.motive: Recently scolded by the host over a breakage
suspects.alibi: In and out of the kitchen carrying trays
suspects.note: Nervous but cooperative.

suspects.name: Gboyega Salami
suspects.role: Host's neighbour who helped tend the fire
suspects.motive: None known
suspects.alibi: Outside by the coal fire most of the afternoon; several guests saw him
suspects.note: Jovial, brought his own cooler of drinks.

findings.heading: Single-pot contamination
findings.detail: Only the pepper-soup pot tested positive. The jollof, the small chops, and all drinks were clean. The pesticide was added late — it sat on top, not fully stirred through.

findings.heading: Container in the kitchen
findings.detail: An empty 50ml veterinary pesticide sachet (brand 'Tikvet') was found in the kitchen bin under tissue, label intact.

findings.heading: Small-chops oil
findings.detail: The caterer's frying oil was over-reused and slightly rancid but harmless — a hygiene issue, not the cause of illness.

findings.heading: Same-day purchase
findings.detail: A single Tikvet 50ml sachet was bought at 1:05pm on the party day, paid by transfer. The sender name on the transfer is 'B. Adeyemi'.

findings.heading: Caterer's tab
findings.detail: Wale Ogunleye bought cooking gas and seasoning at the same shop at 11am — receipt is itemised, no pesticide on it.

witnesses.name: Mama Tope
witnesses.relation: Guest, sat near the kitchen door
witnesses.statement: "I saw the small server girl go to the pepper-soup pot alone around 2 o'clock and stir it, even though it was Folake's pot. I thought she was tasting."

witnesses.name: Kunle
witnesses.statement: "It must be that caterer man, Wale — everyone knows he hates Folake. He was shouting about her this morning."

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Did you go near the pepper-soup pot?

lines.speaker: Bisi Adeyemi
lines.text: Only to carry it to serve. Maybe I stirred it once.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: A transfer of yours bought a sachet of Tikvet pesticide at 1:05pm that day. Why?

lines.speaker: Bisi Adeyemi
lines.text: That... my landlord asked me to buy rat poison for the compound.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Tikvet is a livestock pesticide, not rat poison. And the empty sachet was in this kitchen's bin.

lines.speaker: Bisi Adeyemi
lines.text: I don't know how it got there.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The host scolded you last week. Were you angry enough to spoil her sister's pot?

lines.speaker: Bisi Adeyemi
lines.text: I only wanted them to be embarrassed, not... I didn't think anyone would be this sick.

timeline.event: Wale buys gas and seasoning (no pesticide) at Mokola Agro
Tikvet pesticide sachet bought by 'B. Adeyemi' transfer
Server seen alone at the pepper-soup pot, stirring
Folake begins serving pepper soup in the parlour
Eight guests who ate pepper soup fall ill

results.query: Transfer sender 'B. Adeyemi' at Mokola Agro 1:05pm

rows.label: Role
rows.value: Hired kitchen server at the party

results.note: The pesticide buyer is the kitchen server, placing means in her hands.

tools.name: Call Log
tools.tagline: Pull the rival caterer's afternoon calls

results.note: Nothing tying Wale to the pot or any pesticide — the obvious rival is a dead end.

tools.name: Crime Database
tools.tagline: Trace the agent to recent local sales

results.note: Only one same-day single-sachet sale exists — it matches the agro-shop record.

explanation: Bisi Adeyemi, the hired kitchen server, poisoned the pepper-soup pot to embarrass the host who had scolded her. Toxicology (r1) narrows the crime to a single pot dosed late with a 'Tikvet' livestock pesticide, with the empty sachet hidden in the kitchen bin. The agro-shop record (r2) shows that sachet was bought at 1:05pm the same day by a 'B. Adeyemi' transfer, and Identity Lookup resolves the sender to Bisi — putting means in her hands. Mama Tope reliably saw her alone at Folake's pot at 2pm. The herrings: Kunle blames rival caterer Wale, and the rancid frying oil looks suspicious — but Wale's receipt has no pesticide, his call log is a dead end, and the oil was merely unhygienic. Folake (serving in the parlour) and neighbour Gboyega (at the fire, seen by guests) are cleared.

title: The Burnt Fabric Stall
brief: A lock-up stall full of imported lace at Wuse Market, Abuja, burned out before dawn. No one was hurt. The fire started inside the locked stall, and the fire service flagged it as deliberate. The stall owner had recently doubled the goods' insurance value.

suspects.name: Nkechi Obi
suspects.motive: Stall was failing; just raised the insurance cover; debts to suppliers
suspects.alibi: Says she was home in Garki, asleep; lives alone
suspects.note: Tearful, but already has the insurance claim form filled out.

suspects.name: Musa Ibrahim
suspects.motive: Owner had reported him for sleeping on duty
suspects.alibi: Patrol app logged him across the market all night
suspects.note: Defensive about the earlier report against him.

suspects.name: Ada Eze
suspects.motive: Long boundary dispute with Nkechi over shared frontage
suspects.alibi: Says she was at a night vigil at her church in Kubwa
suspects.note: Coldly satisfied the stall is gone.

suspects.name: Emeka Obi
suspects.motive: Holds a spare key; owed back-pay by his aunt
suspects.alibi: Says he closed up at 8pm and went to a viewing centre
suspects.note: Avoids eye contact when keys are mentioned.

findings.heading: Accelerant pour pattern
findings.detail: Kerosene pour patterns run along the shelving from the back wall. The fire began inside, behind a locked roller door — whoever set it had a key.

findings.heading: Goods already gone
findings.detail: Despite an insured stock of full lace bales, the burnt remains show far fewer bales than claimed. Much of the stock was removed before the fire.

findings.heading: Faulty wiring nearby
findings.detail: An overloaded adapter in the next stall had scorch marks from a previous minor incident — unrelated to this fire's origin point.

findings.heading: Pre-fire stock transfer
findings.detail: A haulage receipt shows 30 bales of lace moved from the stall to a private lock-up in Nyanya two nights before the fire, signed for by the stall owner's phone OTP.

findings.heading: Nephew's back-pay
findings.detail: The nephew was owed ₦60,000; this was paid in full the day after the fire by the owner — looks suspicious but the transfer memo reads 'salary arrears Q2'.

witnesses.name: Alhaji Sani
witnesses.relation: Tea seller at the market entrance
witnesses.statement: "The owner's own car came around 1am, two nights before the fire, and they loaded plenty bales into a small lorry. She said she was restocking elsewhere."

witnesses.name: Patience
witnesses.statement: "That competitor Ada has wanted Nkechi's stall finished for years. Check her, not anybody else."

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You raised your insurance cover to ₦9.5m eleven days before the fire. Why then?

lines.speaker: Nkechi Obi
lines.text: Business was growing. I wanted proper cover. Is that a crime?

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The fire service found far fewer bales than you insured. Where is the rest of the stock?

lines.speaker: Nkechi Obi
lines.text: Everything burned. Everything.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: A haulage receipt and a tea seller both say 30 bales left your stall to a Nyanya lock-up two nights before, signed by your phone OTP.

lines.speaker: Nkechi Obi
lines.text: That... I was just moving slow-selling goods.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: And the stall, locked, with no forced entry, was set alight from inside with kerosene. Who else has a key?

lines.speaker: Nkechi Obi
lines.text: Only me and my nephew. I... I want a lawyer.

timeline.event: Insurance cover raised ₦4.2m to ₦9.5m
30 bales loaded from stall to Nyanya lock-up via owner OTP
Nephew closes stall, goes to viewing centre
Fire detected inside locked stall; no forced entry
Owner submits ₦9.5m insurance claim

tools.name: Phone Records
tools.tagline: Verify which line signed the haulage OTP

results.note: The stock-removal receipt was authorised by the owner's own phone.

tools.name: Cell Triangulation
tools.tagline: Place a phone at fire time

results.note: Her phone was at the market during the fire, not asleep at home — alibi broken.

rows.label: Result
rows.value: Phone idle at the Kubwa church mast all night

results.note: The obvious enemy lead goes nowhere.

explanation: Nkechi Obi, the stall owner, committed insurance arson. Eleven days before the fire she nearly doubled her cover, then two nights before quietly moved 30 bales of lace to a Nyanya lock-up — a transfer the haulage OTP and Phone Records tie to her own line, and that the reliable tea seller witnessed (r2, w1). The fire was set from inside with kerosene behind a locked roller door with no forced entry (r1), meaning the arsonist had a key, and far fewer bales remained than insured because she had removed the good stock. Triangulation places her phone at the Wuse Market mast during the fire while she claimed to be asleep in Garki. The herrings: hostile witness Patience points at competitor Ada, but Ada's call log is a dead end (idle at her church) and her alibi confirmed; the nephew's back-pay and the faulty wiring next door are also misdirection. Security guard Musa (patrol-app confirmed) and Ada are cleared.

title: The Ghost Worker on the Payroll
brief: An NGO in Port Harcourt discovered a salary going monthly to a field officer, 'Joy Wike', who no one in the office has ever met. Over a year, ₦3.6m left the payroll to her account. Only a few staff could add a name to the payroll system.

suspects.name: Tamuno George
suspects.motive: Controls payroll entries and approves disbursements
suspects.alibi: Login credentials are personal, but says he shares the office PC
suspects.note: Quick to point at the IT contractor.

suspects.name: Ibim Briggs
suspects.motive: Signs off the payroll batch each month
suspects.alibi: Travels constantly; was abroad three of the months in question
suspects.note: Demands the fraud be traced to the naira.

suspects.name: Sayuni Daniel
suspects.motive: Handles staff onboarding paperwork
suspects.alibi: Was on three months' maternity leave in the middle of the period
suspects.note: Co-operative, brings her own records.

suspects.name: Chetachi Amadi
suspects.motive: Has system admin access; underpaid contract
suspects.alibi: On-site only on Tuesdays per his contract log
suspects.note: Insists he 'only fixes the network, never touches payroll'.

findings.heading: Who created the record
findings.detail: The 'Joy Wike' staff record was created and edited only from Tamuno George's personal login, including on dates when no one else was in finance. Each monthly amount was also keyed under that same login.

findings.heading: Admin access myth
findings.detail: The IT contractor's admin account can reset passwords but is blocked from creating payroll staff records by design — he could not have added 'Joy Wike'.

findings.heading: Approval auto-pass
findings.detail: Three of the monthly batches were auto-approved while the director was abroad, using a delegated approval the finance officer himself configured.

findings.heading: Same phone, same address
findings.detail: The 'Joy Wike' bank account was opened with the same registered phone number and residential address as Tamuno George's personal account.

findings.heading: Onward sweep
findings.detail: Each salary credit to 'Joy Wike' was swept within 48 hours to Tamuno George's personal account or used to pay his car loan.

findings.heading: HR's separate error
findings.detail: The HR assistant once double-entered a real staffer's bonus by mistake; it was caught and reversed — unrelated to the ghost worker.

witnesses.name: Grace
witnesses.statement: "Nobody called Joy Wike ever signed our visitors' book or collected an ID card. There is no such person in this office."

witnesses.name: Bright
witnesses.statement: "It has to be the IT man. He can do anything on those computers — he has the admin password."

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who is Joy Wike?

lines.speaker: Tamuno George
lines.text: A field officer. Maybe she works remotely. I just process the payroll.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Her record was created only from your login, sometimes when you were alone in finance.

lines.speaker: Tamuno George
lines.text: We share that PC. Anyone could have used my login. Ask the IT contractor.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The IT account is blocked from creating payroll records. And Joy Wike's bank account was opened with your phone number and your home address.

lines.speaker: Tamuno George
lines.text: That... must be a bank error.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Her salary was swept into your account and paid your car loan, every month for a year.

lines.speaker: Tamuno George
lines.text: I think I need to call my lawyer.

timeline.event: 'Joy Wike' record created from Tamuno's login
Salary keyed and batch sent under Tamuno's login
Director abroad; batch auto-approved via finance officer's delegation
'Joy Wike' funds swept to Tamuno's account / car loan
HR assistant on maternity leave; payments continued anyway

results.note: The ghost worker's account is registered to Tamuno George's own phone and address.

tools.name: Phone Records
tools.tagline: Match the beneficiary number to a person

results.note: One phone number, one person — the ghost worker is Tamuno.

tools.name: Crime Database
tools.tagline: Check the IT contractor for prior fraud

results.note: No record, and his admin role can't create payroll staff — the IT lead goes nowhere.

explanation: Tamuno George, the finance officer, ran a ghost-worker payroll fraud. The audit log (r1) shows the fictitious 'Joy Wike' record was created and edited only from his personal login, and three batches were auto-approved through a delegation he configured while the director was abroad. The decisive proof is r2: the 'Joy Wike' account was opened with Tamuno's own phone number and home address, and every salary was swept within 48 hours into his account or onto his car loan. Identity Lookup and Phone Records both resolve the beneficiary number to Tamuno. Reliable witness Grace confirms no such person existed. The herrings: Bright and Tamuno blame the IT contractor Chetachi, but his admin account is by-design blocked from creating payroll records and his crime-db check is a dead end; HR's unrelated double-bonus error is also misdirection. The director (abroad) and HR assistant (on maternity leave) are cleared.

title: The Cut Fibre Cable
brief: An internet provider's main fibre line in Enugu was severed overnight, knocking out service to half the city and a hospital. It was no accident — the cable was deliberately cut and a section removed. A rival contractor had just lost a renewal bid to the affected company.

suspects.name: Obinna Eze
suspects.motive: Lost the city renewal contract; public bitterness
suspects.alibi: Says he was at a bar in Independence Layout till late; friends confirm till 11pm
suspects.note: Smirks, says 'maybe rats chewed it'.

suspects.name: Chinedu Okafor
suspects.motive: Passed over for promotion; knows the cable route exactly
suspects.alibi: On-call that night; phone GPS shows him on the route
suspects.note: Says he was 'just checking the line' when it failed.

suspects.name: Ngozi Udeh
suspects.role: Local landowner over the cable easement
suspects.motive: Disputes the easement; wants the line rerouted off her land
suspects.alibi: At a family burial in Nsukka all weekend; many attendees
suspects.note: Angry about the cable but was two hours away.

suspects.name: Sunday Agu
suspects.motive: Copper/fibre scrap value
suspects.alibi: Says he was at the scrap depot; depot logs him in next morning only
suspects.note: Twitchy when copper is mentioned.

header.label: Tool
header.value: Professional fibre cutter, not scrap shears

findings.heading: Specialist cut
findings.detail: The cut was made with a calibrated fibre splicing/cutting tool and the junction was opened correctly first — the work of someone who knows fibre, not a random scrap thief hacking at it.

findings.heading: Removed section abandoned nearby
findings.detail: The 6m section was found dumped in bushes 40m away, not taken for scrap — ruling out theft-for-resale as the motive.

findings.heading: Old machete marks
findings.detail: Separate, older machete nicks on the outer duct are from past bush-clearing — unrelated to the clean cut.

findings.heading: Maintenance code used
findings.detail: The fibre junction box was opened at 1:18am using technician Chinedu Okafor's personal maintenance access code — no work ticket was raised for that night.

findings.heading: GPS on the route
findings.detail: Chinedu's company phone GPS places him at the exact cut location from 1:05am to 1:40am, bracketing the cut.

findings.heading: Rival's quote spike
findings.detail: Rival contractor Obinna emailed the city an 'emergency repair' quote at 9am — opportunistic, but sent after the outage was already public news.

witnesses.name: Okwy
witnesses.statement: "I saw one provider-uniform man with a tool bag crouched at the junction box around 1 in the morning. I thought he was fixing it."

witnesses.name: Mama Chidi
witnesses.statement: "That Obinna was drinking in front of my kiosk that night. He hates the other company — it must be him."

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You were on-call. Did you visit the junction at Trans-Ekulu that night?

lines.speaker: Chinedu Okafor
lines.text: Yes, I was checking the line. It was already failing.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: There was no work ticket. The junction was opened at 1:18am with your personal code.

lines.speaker: Chinedu Okafor
lines.text: I... opened it to inspect. That's allowed for on-call.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The cut was made with a calibrated fibre tool by someone who knows the route. Your GPS has you there from 1:05 to 1:40am.

lines.speaker: Chinedu Okafor
lines.text: Anyone could be there.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You were passed over for promotion last month. The section was cut and thrown in the bush, not stolen. This was a message, not a theft.

lines.speaker: Chinedu Okafor
lines.text: I won't say anything more without a lawyer.

timeline.event: Chinedu passed over for promotion
Rival Obinna seen drinking at Independence Layout bar
Chinedu's GPS arrives at the cut location
Junction opened with Chinedu's maintenance code; no ticket
GPS leaves; 6m section later found dumped in bushes nearby

tools.name: Phone Records
tools.tagline: Tie the maintenance code and GPS to a person

results.note: The code that opened the junction and the on-site phone both belong to Chinedu.

tools.name: Cell Triangulation
tools.tagline: Place the rival at cut time

results.note: Obinna's phone never reaches the cut site — the obvious rival is in the clear.

tools.name: Crime Database
tools.tagline: Check the scrap collector for cable theft

results.note: No record, and the section was abandoned not sold — the scrap-theft angle is a dead end.

explanation: Chinedu Okafor, the provider's own field technician, sabotaged the fibre line out of resentment at being passed over for promotion. The forensic report (r1) shows a specialist clean cut made with a calibrated fibre tool by someone who knew the route — not a scrap thief — and the removed section was dumped nearby rather than taken, proving the motive was sabotage, not theft. The key evidence (r2) shows the junction was opened at 1:18am with Chinedu's personal maintenance code with no work ticket, while his GPS brackets him at the cut site from 1:05 to 1:40am; Phone Records tie both the code and device to him, and the okada rider Okwy reliably saw a uniformed man with a tool bag at the box at 1am. The herrings: Mama Chidi and the rival's opportunistic 9am repair quote point at contractor Obinna, but Triangulation places Obinna at the bar and home, never at the cut site. The scrap collector Sunday is a dead end, and landowner Ngozi was confirmed at a Nsukka burial.

title: The Ransom Drop at Mile 2
brief: Twelve-year-old Ebube Okafor was taken from the gate of Brightstars Academy in Surulere, Lagos, on a Tuesday afternoon. The family paid ₦4.5 million at a drop point near Mile 2, and Ebube was released unharmed the next morning. The money is gone, but the boy says he was kept in a quiet room and heard a generator and a familiar bell. The police suspect the abduction was arranged by someone close to the family.

suspects.name: Chuka Okafor
suspects.role: the boy's father, owns a tiles business
suspects.motive: business heavily in debt; insurance and sympathy could clear suppliers
suspects.alibi: says he was at his Ikeja showroom all afternoon, confirmed by two staff and CCTV
suspects.note: wept openly; hands shook when the ransom call recording was played

suspects.name: Ngozi Eze
suspects.motive: owed ₦600,000 to a thrift collective; recently asked Chuka for a loan and was refused
suspects.alibi: claims she went to the market at Aguda during the school run, no receipt
suspects.note: calm, almost rehearsed; corrected the detective on the exact pickup time

suspects.name: Bode Adeyemi
suspects.motive: underpaid; gambling debts at a betting kiosk nearby
suspects.alibi: logged in the gate register but left post for 25 minutes, says he went to buy fuel for the gen
suspects.note: sweated, blamed the school for short-staffing the gate

suspects.name: Halima Bello
suspects.motive: none obvious; close to the family, godmother to Ebube's sister
suspects.alibi: teaching a JSS2 class until 3:10pm, confirmed by the school timetable and pupils
suspects.note: distraught but coherent; offered her own savings to help

reports.title: Ransom Call & SIM Analysis
reports.subtitle: Lagos State Police Anti-Kidnapping Unit / Wed

findings.heading: Background sound
findings.detail: The second ransom call carries a distinct recurring church bell chime every few seconds, plus a steady generator hum. The cadence matches a Catholic Angelus chime.

findings.heading: Tower location
findings.detail: All three ransom calls pinged the same mast in the Aguda/Coker area of Surulere, within roughly 800m of St. Dominic's Catholic Church.

findings.heading: Burner purchased nearby
findings.detail: The burner SIM was activated the morning of the abduction. Top-up was a ₦500 voucher bought at a kiosk in Aguda market.

findings.heading: Father's number flagged
findings.detail: Chuka's personal line exchanged 14 calls with a loan-shark number in the prior week, unrelated to the kidnapping.

findings.heading: Cement dust on clothes
findings.detail: Ebube's shirt carried fine grey cement dust and red-sand residue consistent with a building-supplies environment.

findings.heading: Footwear soil
findings.detail: Soil in the room's threshold matched the unpaved compound of a residential block behind St. Dominic's, not the father's tiled showroom.

findings.heading: Snack wrapper
findings.detail: A wrapper for a brand of biscuit sold mainly at Aguda market was found where Ebube was held.

findings.heading: Nanny's debt cleared
findings.detail: Ngozi Eze paid off ₦600,000 to her thrift collective in two cash deposits the day after the ransom was collected.

findings.heading: Guard's betting
findings.detail: Bode Adeyemi placed and lost small bets totalling ₦18,000 the same week; no large inflow appears in his account.

findings.heading: Father's overdraft
findings.detail: Chuka's business account is ₦9m overdrawn; no portion of the ₦4.5m ransom returned to him.

witnesses.name: Sister Mary-Jane
witnesses.statement: A woman rented the back room of our old catechist block last week, said it was for storing market goods. She paid cash and came and went with a covered bag.

witnesses.name: Tunde the gateman's friend
witnesses.statement: Bode owes everybody. He was here the afternoon that boy was taken, shouting at the football scores. He never left for long.

witnesses.name: Ebube Okafor
witnesses.statement: I heard a bell, ding, ding, like in church. And a generator. The person who brought me rice had soft hands and smelled like the powder Auntie uses.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Where were you during the 2:45 school run?

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: Aguda market. Buying foodstuff for the house.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Any receipt, or someone who saw you?

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: You don't get receipts in Aguda market, officer.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You cleared a ₦600,000 debt the day after the ransom. Where from?

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: My brother abroad sent it. He sends money sometimes.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: In two cash deposits, no transfer trail?

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: He sends it through someone. I cannot explain everything.

timeline.event: Ebube leaves class for the gate
Bode logs out of the gate post to buy fuel
Ebube taken from the gate
Ngozi says she is at Aguda market
First ransom call, Aguda mast
Ransom collected at Mile 2
Ebube released on Eric Moore St, Aguda

tools.name: Mast Triangulation
tools.tagline: Pin the calls to a footprint

results.note: Tight cluster, no movement during calls.
Caretaker recognised her photo from the family album.
Debt calls, but no link to the abduction.
Both deposited day after ransom; no inbound transfer.

explanation: The nanny, Ngozi Eze, arranged the kidnapping. The ransom call's church-bell chime and generator hum (r1) place the hideout within 800m of St. Dominic's in Aguda, where the caretaker (w1) identifies Ngozi as the woman who rented the back room days earlier with cash. The release soil and snack wrapper (r2) confirm Aguda, and Ebube's memory of 'soft hands' and familiar powder points to a carer, not a labourer. The clincher is money: she cleared a ₦600,000 debt in two unexplained cash deposits the day after the drop (r3/Account Trace), with a fabricated 'brother abroad' story and no transfer trail. The father is cleared by confirmed showroom CCTV; his loan-shark calls were a herring. The guard's broken alibi and gambling debts (and the cement-dust herring suggesting a building site) mislead, but his betting-kiosk friend places him locally with no large inflow. The teacher was teaching all afternoon.

title: Poison in the Pepper Soup
brief: Chief Adamu Sani, a wealthy contractor, collapsed and died after a family dinner at his home in Maitama, Abuja. The last thing he ate was catfish pepper soup. The autopsy finds a toxic dose of an organophosphate pesticide. Four people shared the table, and the Chief had just announced he was changing his will.

suspects.name: Amina Sani
suspects.motive: stood to lose her share under the announced new will
suspects.alibi: served the soup; in the kitchen and dining room all evening
suspects.note: composed, kept steering questions to the cook

suspects.name: Emeka Sani
suspects.role: the Chief's son from his first marriage
suspects.motive: deep gambling debts; the new will favoured a charity over him
suspects.alibi: says he stepped out to take a call on the veranda during dinner
suspects.note: defensive, smelled of cigarettes, fidgeted with his phone

suspects.name: Grace Udo
suspects.motive: recently scolded and threatened with dismissal over missing housekeeping money
suspects.alibi: prepared the soup in the kitchen; tasted it herself at 6pm
suspects.note: frightened, insisted the soup was clean when it left her pot

suspects.name: Dr. Yusuf Lawal
suspects.role: the Chief's personal physician and dinner guest
suspects.motive: the Chief was auditing medical-retainer payments he'd been overbilling
suspects.alibi: at the table the whole time; first to attempt CPR
suspects.note: clinical, quick to call it a cardiac event before tests returned

findings.heading: Toxin identified
findings.detail: Stomach contents show a concentrated organophosphate pesticide, the kind sold as a grain-storage powder in open markets. Dose was far above accidental contamination.

findings.heading: Localised to last course
findings.detail: Toxin concentration was highest in residue consistent with the pepper soup, ingested only minutes before collapse.

findings.heading: No prior heart disease
findings.detail: Coronary arteries were clear; there was no cardiac event, contradicting the early on-scene assessment.

findings.heading: Mild ulcer
findings.detail: An unrelated, long-standing gastric ulcer was noted, not contributory.

findings.heading: Communal pot clean
findings.detail: The main soup pot and Grace's tasting ladle tested negative for the pesticide. The soup was clean when it left the kitchen.

findings.heading: One bowl positive
findings.detail: Only the Chief's individual serving bowl tested positive, indicating the toxin was added after plating, at the table.

findings.heading: Pesticide tin in pantry
findings.detail: An open tin of grain-storage pesticide sat on a low pantry shelf, accessible to household staff and family alike.

findings.heading: Cook's fingerprints on tin
findings.detail: Grace's prints were on the pesticide tin, consistent with her routine storage duties.

findings.heading: Son not on veranda
findings.detail: Emeka claimed he was on the veranda taking a call, but his phone shows no call during dinner and stayed connected to the dining-room WiFi access point the whole time.

findings.heading: Wife seated beside Chief
findings.detail: The reconstructed seating places Amina to the Chief's immediate right; she plated and passed him his bowl personally.

findings.heading: Wife's search history
findings.detail: Amina's phone shows searches three days earlier for 'how fast does grain pesticide work' and 'symptoms organophosphate', deleted from history but recovered.

findings.heading: Doctor's billing texts
findings.detail: Dr. Lawal exchanged texts about 'sorting the retainer audit quietly' with an accountant the week before.

witnesses.name: Halima
witnesses.statement: Madam Amina would not let me carry Oga's bowl. She said she always serves him herself, that is their tradition. She held his bowl a moment by the sideboard before placing it.

witnesses.name: Emeka Sani
witnesses.statement: I was outside on the veranda the whole time, on a long call. I didn't even eat the soup. Ask anyone. The cook has been stealing for months, look at her.

witnesses.name: Grace Udo
witnesses.statement: I tasted that soup myself at six o'clock, as I always do. If it had poison I would be dead too. I touch that tin every week for the rice, that is all.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who served the Chief his pepper soup?

lines.speaker: Amina
lines.text: I did. I always serve my husband myself. It is our custom.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The pot was clean, but only his bowl had pesticide in it.

lines.speaker: Amina
lines.text: Then someone tampered after I plated it. Ask the cook, she handles that poison.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Three days ago your phone searched how fast grain pesticide works.

lines.speaker: Amina
lines.text: That... I was worried about rats in the pantry. That is all.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You deleted the searches the same night.

lines.speaker: Amina
lines.text: I delete things all the time. This proves nothing.

timeline.event: Grace tastes the finished soup, no ill effect
Dinner begins; Amina plates and passes the Chief's bowl
Emeka claims he left for a veranda call
Chief eats the pepper soup
Chief collapses
Dr. Lawal calls it a cardiac event
Chief pronounced dead at National Hospital

results.note: Change announced at the very dinner; not yet signed.
Three days before the dinner.
His alibi is false, but he never touched the Chief's bowl.
Matches pantry tin and the 20-minute collapse.

explanation: Amina Sani poisoned her husband. The autopsy (r1) confirms organophosphate, not a heart attack, and the swabs (r2, the key report) prove the communal pot and tasting ladle were clean while only the Chief's individual bowl was positive, meaning the toxin was added at the table after plating. The steward (w1) and seating reconstruction (r3) establish that Amina alone plated and handled his bowl by the sideboard, as was her insisted custom. Her recovered, then-deleted search 'how fast does grain pesticide work' three days prior, plus her motive (the new will stripping her 40% share, per the will lookup), seal it. The cook is cleared: she tasted the same clean pot at 6pm and survived, and her prints on the tin and the accessible pantry pesticide are herrings designed to frame her. The son lied about the veranda call (his phone never left the dining-room WiFi), but he never handled the Chief's bowl, so his false alibi is a herring. The doctor's overbilling and premature 'cardiac' call mislead but don't connect to the poisoned bowl.

title: The Burnt Warehouse at Trade Fair
brief: A textile warehouse at the Lagos International Trade Fair Complex burned down overnight, destroying goods insured for ₦80 million. The fire started near the rear store, and investigators find traces of an accelerant. The night watchman was found locked out, and the owner had recently doubled the insurance cover.

suspects.name: Rasheed Olaniyan
suspects.motive: stock wasn't selling; he doubled the insurance cover two weeks before the fire
suspects.alibi: says he was at home in Lekki, phone pinged Lekki mast all night
suspects.note: calm, almost relieved; talked about the payout before the cause was known

suspects.name: Sikiru Bello
suspects.motive: owed three months' salary; recently quarrelled with the owner
suspects.alibi: found locked outside the gate; says he was shut out when the side door jammed
suspects.note: smelled faintly of smoke, agitated, kept saying he was set up

suspects.name: Patience Nwankwo
suspects.role: a rival textile trader in the next block
suspects.motive: long-running feud over stall allocations and undercut prices
suspects.alibi: claims she travelled to Aba that day for stock; bus ticket produced
suspects.note: openly hostile, said the owner 'deserved' the loss

suspects.name: Femi Adebayo
suspects.motive: paid per call-out; recently flagged faulty wiring the owner refused to fix
suspects.alibi: did a rewiring job in the rear store that afternoon; left at 5pm per gate log
suspects.note: cooperative, worried the fire would be blamed on his wiring

findings.heading: Pour pattern
findings.detail: A deliberate petrol pour pattern runs along the rear store floor and up a stack of fabric bales. This was set, not electrical.

findings.heading: Electrical ruled out
findings.detail: The rear-store wiring, recently redone, was intact and de-energised at the breaker. The fire did not start at the wiring.

findings.heading: Side door wedged
findings.detail: The side fire door was wedged shut from the inside with a metal bar, which is why the watchman could not re-enter. Someone inside sealed it before lighting up.

findings.heading: Jerry can residue
findings.detail: A plastic jerry can with petrol residue was found in a drainage ditch outside the rear fence.

findings.heading: Owner stayed in Lekki
findings.detail: Rasheed's phone pinged a Lekki mast continuously from 9pm to dawn, with no movement toward the Trade Fair complex.

findings.heading: Watchman locked out at 11:50
findings.detail: Front-gate CCTV shows Sikiru outside the gate from 11:50pm, hammering on it, well before the fire is first seen at 12:25am.

findings.heading: Figure at rear fence
findings.detail: A grainy rear camera caught a person climbing out over the rear fence at 12:20am, carrying a can-shaped object, minutes before the blaze.

findings.heading: Rival's bus to Aba
findings.detail: Patience's phone last pinged a Mile 12 mast at 6pm, consistent with an outbound Aba trip; no Trade Fair pings overnight.

findings.heading: Cover doubled
findings.detail: Rasheed raised the policy from ₦40m to ₦80m two weeks before the fire, well above the actual stock value.

findings.heading: Cash to handyman
findings.detail: Rasheed transferred ₦450,000 to Femi Adebayo three days before the fire, labelled 'extra job'. The rewiring invoice was only ₦120,000.

findings.heading: Watchman unpaid
findings.detail: Sikiru was indeed owed three months' wages; no unusual inflows to his account.

witnesses.name: Alhaji Musa
witnesses.statement: I saw the watchman banging on the front gate before midnight, before any fire. Whoever did this was already inside and would not open for him.

witnesses.name: Patience Nwankwo
witnesses.statement: I was in Aba, ask the motor park. But I will not pretend to cry. Rasheed is a cheat and that fire is karma. Maybe he burned it himself for the money.

witnesses.name: Chidi
witnesses.relation: okada rider near the rear fence
witnesses.statement: Around past twelve, a man jumped the back fence with a keg and ran toward the canal. Not tall, work boots, looked like one of the staff. I dropped my passenger and went, I didn't wait.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The gate log says you left at 5pm. Did you return?

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: No. I finished the rewiring and went home.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Rasheed paid you ₦450,000 but the rewiring invoice was ₦120,000. Explain the rest.

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: He said it was advance for future jobs. He is a generous man.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: A man in work boots was seen jumping the rear fence with a petrol can at 12:20am.

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: Plenty people wear work boots. That is not me.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You know the rear fire door and where the petrol store is. You wedged that door from inside.

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: I... I want a lawyer now.

timeline.event: Femi logs out after rewiring the rear store
Sikiru locked outside front gate, banging
Side fire door found later wedged from inside
Figure climbs rear fence with a can
Fire first visible at rear store
Watchman still outside as fire spreads
Owner's phone steady on Lekki mast

results.note: Owner physically absent; he didn't light it himself.
₦330,000 unaccounted, 3 days pre-fire.
Matches handyman build and the okada rider's account.
Trip checks out; not at the scene.

explanation: The handyman Femi Adebayo set the fire, paid by the owner. The fire forensics (r1) prove deliberate arson, a petrol pour pattern, and crucially that the side fire door was wedged shut from the inside, meaning the arsonist was inside the locked warehouse, which is why the watchman was stuck outside banging from 11:50pm (front CCTV, r2). The rear camera and okada rider (w3, CCTV pull) show a short figure in work boots climbing the rear fence with a petrol can at 12:20am, minutes before ignition, matching Femi, who knew the rear store, the petrol store, and the fire-door layout from his rewiring job that day. The money confirms it: Rasheed paid Femi ₦450,000 against a ₦120,000 invoice three days prior (r3/Account Trace). The owner is cleared as the lighter, his phone never left Lekki, though he financed it. The doubled insurance and intact 'electrical' wiring are herrings pointing at owner-as-arsonist and accidental cause. The hostile rival's Aba trip checks out, and the unpaid watchman, the obvious framed suspect, was demonstrably locked out before the fire.

title: The Vanishing Cooperative Funds
brief: The Ireti Multipurpose Cooperative in Ibadan discovers ₦12 million missing from its members' savings account. The chairman calls it a hack; the bank insists every withdrawal used valid credentials and OTPs. Four officers held access. The money moved out in small, frequent transfers over three months.

suspects.name: Tijani Akande
suspects.motive: lifestyle beyond means; building a house in Oluyole
suspects.alibi: says he rarely touched the online portal, left it to the treasurer
suspects.note: loud, blamed hackers, demanded the bank refund everyone

suspects.name: Folake Ogunde
suspects.motive: had primary portal access; sole signatory on small transfers
suspects.alibi: logged in daily for legitimate dues; says her password was stolen
suspects.note: tearful, kept saying she would never steal from her own people

suspects.name: Kunle Bamidele
suspects.motive: set up the online portal; knows the security questions
suspects.alibi: claims he only did setup once and never logged in after
suspects.note: cocky, used jargon to deflect, mentioned 'phishing' unprompted

suspects.name: Bisi Akande
suspects.role: the chairman's wife and a senior member
suspects.motive: co-owner of the Oluyole house project; benefits from any inflow
suspects.alibi: no portal access; was running her shop at Bodija all quarter
suspects.note: indignant, insisted she handles no cooperative money

findings.heading: Salami pattern
findings.detail: Funds left in 47 transfers of ₦200,000-₦300,000 each, just under internal review thresholds, to a web of new wallets.

findings.heading: Funnel account
findings.detail: Every wallet eventually swept into one settlement account at a fintech, registered to a BVN belonging to the IT volunteer Kunle Bamidele.

findings.heading: Chairman's house deposits
findings.detail: Tijani paid a ₦3m deposit on the Oluyole house during the same period, sourced from a verifiable land sale, not the cooperative.

findings.heading: Valid OTPs
findings.detail: Every withdrawal used a valid OTP sent to the registered portal phone number.

findings.heading: OTP number changed
findings.detail: The portal's registered OTP phone number was quietly switched to a new SIM 4 days before the first theft, then switched back after the last. The change request came from the admin profile.

findings.heading: Admin profile is IT volunteer's
findings.detail: The admin profile that could alter the registered OTP number was Kunle's setup account, which he claimed never to use after install.

findings.heading: Treasurer's logins routine
findings.detail: Folake's daily logins came from her usual phone and matched legitimate dues entries; none coincided with the 47 theft transfers.

findings.heading: One login from cyber café
findings.detail: A single login traced to a Dugbe cyber café used the treasurer's credentials.

findings.heading: OTP SIM in one phone
findings.detail: The new OTP SIM and the fintech funnel account were accessed from the same single handset throughout the three months.

findings.heading: Handset is Kunle's
findings.detail: That handset's IMEI matches a phone Kunle registered to his own line for an unrelated subscription.

findings.heading: Café CCTV
findings.detail: Dugbe café CCTV on the date of the treasurer-credential login shows a man, not Folake, at that terminal.

witnesses.name: Mama Risi
witnesses.statement: Kunle set up everything and said only he understood it. When members complained, he talked computer-computer until everybody was confused and went home.

witnesses.name: Kunle Bamidele
witnesses.statement: I set it up once, that's all. This is clearly phishing, the treasurer's password leaked. I haven't logged in since installation. Check the treasurer, not me.

witnesses.name: Dauda
witnesses.statement: That seat? A young man used it that day, he paid cash, fast typer. Not a woman. He comes around sometimes.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You said you never logged in after setup. The admin profile changed the OTP number twice.

lines.speaker: Kunle
lines.text: Anyone could have used the admin profile. I shared the setup details.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The new OTP SIM and the funnel account ran from one phone. Its IMEI is yours.

lines.speaker: Kunle
lines.text: I lost that phone months ago. Someone must have used it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You reported no loss, and the fintech account is on your BVN.

lines.speaker: Kunle
lines.text: BVNs get cloned. This is a setup against the IT guy, always the IT guy.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The café CCTV shows a man at the treasurer's login, not Folake.

lines.speaker: Kunle
lines.text: So now any man in Ibadan is me?

timeline.event: Portal OTP number switched via admin profile
First salami transfer leaves the savings account
Single café login on treasurer's credentials
Chairman pays ₦3m house deposit from land sale
Last salami transfer; wallets sweep to fintech
OTP number switched back to original
Members notice shortfall; chairman cries hack

results.note: All 47 transfers swept here.
One phone ran both SIM and theft account.
House money is clean; not from the theft.
Treasurer's credentials used by a man.

explanation: The IT volunteer Kunle Bamidele embezzled the funds. The key is the portal access log (r2): the registered OTP phone number was quietly switched to a new SIM four days before the first theft and switched back after the last, and only the admin setup profile, which was Kunle's, could make that change, despite his claim he never logged in after install. With OTPs diverted to a SIM he controlled, every 'valid OTP' withdrawal (r1) was his. Forensics (r3) and the tools tie one handset, by IMEI, to both that OTP SIM and the fintech funnel account, which sits on Kunle's own BVN and received all 47 swept transfers. The treasurer is cleared: her logins were routine, never coincided with theft transfers, and the café login on her credentials was a man (CCTV, w3), a deliberate frame, the herring meant to pin it on her. The chairman's ₦3m house deposit looks damning but traces to a documented land sale (Identity Lookup, dead end), and his wife had no access at all.

title: Sabotage at the Bottling Line
brief: At a soft-drinks plant in Port Harcourt, the main bottling line seizes mid-shift, shattering thousands of bottles and halting production for days, costing roughly ₦15 million. Engineers find a steel bolt deliberately dropped into the conveyor gearbox. Access to the gearbox is restricted, and the plant had just announced layoffs.

suspects.name: Ifeanyi Obi
suspects.motive: on the layoff list; bitter about being replaced by automation
suspects.alibi: says he was on the line floor doing routine checks during the seizure
suspects.note: angry, openly resented management, said 'they had it coming'

suspects.name: Tamuno West
suspects.motive: missed targets; a sabotage claim could excuse his quarter and trigger insurance
suspects.alibi: in his office on a video call with HQ during the seizure, call logged
suspects.note: polished, quick to call it deliberate before engineers confirmed

suspects.name: Blessing Amadi
suspects.motive: passed over for a promotion that went to a junior; key-card to restricted zones
suspects.alibi: says she was in the QC lab testing samples; lab has no camera
suspects.note: quiet, evasive about her movements, fidgeted with her key-card lanyard

suspects.name: Sunday Eze
suspects.motive: sacked two weeks earlier; held a grudge; knew the plant layout
suspects.alibi: claims he was at home in Diobu; no longer has plant access
suspects.note: resentful, said the plant 'used and dumped' him

findings.heading: Deliberate insertion
findings.detail: The 16mm bolt was dropped through the gearbox inspection hatch, which is normally bolted shut and only opened for servicing. This was no stray part.

findings.heading: Hatch opened with key-card
findings.detail: The gearbox hatch interlock is released by a restricted key-card. Logs show one card released it at 1:42pm, 11 minutes before the seizure.

findings.heading: Bolt type not from line stock
findings.detail: The bolt is a 16mm type used in the QC lab's equipment mounts, not the conveyor's own 12mm fasteners.

findings.heading: Lubricant contamination
findings.detail: Old gearbox oil also showed metal shavings from ordinary long-term wear, unrelated to the bolt.

findings.heading: Card at hatch
findings.detail: The card that released the gearbox hatch at 1:42pm was issued to QC inspector Blessing Amadi.

findings.heading: Engineer elsewhere
findings.detail: Ifeanyi Obi's card pinged the far packaging dock at 1:40pm, away from the gearbox, contradicting his claim he was beside the line.

findings.heading: Fired cleaner not on site
findings.detail: Sunday Eze's deactivated card shows no entry; gate CCTV confirms he never entered the premises.

findings.heading: Manager on HQ call
findings.detail: Tamuno's office line carried a continuous video call with HQ from 1:30 to 2:15pm.

findings.heading: Inspector's grievance
findings.detail: Blessing filed a written grievance days earlier after a junior got the promotion she expected, threatening to 'make them regret it'.

findings.heading: Inspector borrowed lab tools
findings.detail: Maintenance log shows Blessing signed out a 16mm bolt set from the QC lab the morning of the incident, never returned.

findings.heading: Engineer's union texts
findings.detail: Ifeanyi sent texts urging colleagues to protest the layoffs, but nothing about sabotage.

findings.heading: Cleaner's threats
findings.detail: Sunday sent two angry texts to a former supervisor saying the plant would 'pay', but had no means of access.

witnesses.name: Engr. Dele
witnesses.statement: Only QC and senior maintenance carry the card that opens that hatch. And that bolt is a lab bolt, not ours. Someone with lab access dropped it.

witnesses.name: Ifeanyi Obi
witnesses.statement: Yes I'm angry, they're sacking us for robots. But I was at the packaging dock, check my card. I'd strike, not sabotage. Look at the cleaner they fired, he's the bitter one.

witnesses.name: Chioma
witnesses.statement: Blessing told me to cover for her if anyone asked, that she was in the lab all afternoon. But she left for a while around half past one and came back flustered.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Where were you at 1:42pm?

lines.speaker: Blessing
lines.text: In the QC lab, testing samples. All afternoon.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Your key-card released the gearbox hatch at exactly 1:42.

lines.speaker: Blessing
lines.text: My card? It must have been cloned, or someone took it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You signed out a 16mm bolt set from the lab that morning. The bolt in the gearbox is a 16mm lab bolt.

lines.speaker: Blessing
lines.text: We sign tools out all the time. That means nothing.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Your colleague says you asked her to cover for you and left the lab around 1:30.

lines.speaker: Blessing
lines.text: She's lying. They all want to blame the woman.

timeline.event: Blessing signs out a 16mm bolt set from QC lab
Blessing leaves the QC lab, asks colleague to cover
Ifeanyi's card pings the packaging dock
Blessing's card releases the gearbox hatch
Bottling line seizes; bottles shatter
Blessing returns to lab, flustered
Manager ends HQ video call

results.note: 11 minutes before the seizure.
Bolt is not from conveyor stock; it's a lab part.
Fired cleaner never came onto the premises.
Manager accounted for throughout the seizure.

explanation: QC inspector Blessing Amadi sabotaged the line. The gearbox failure analysis (r1, key report) establishes the bolt was deliberately dropped through a hatch that only a restricted key-card opens, and that the bolt is a 16mm QC-lab part, not a 12mm conveyor fastener. The access log (r2) shows her card released that hatch at 1:42pm, eleven minutes before the seizure, while she claimed to be in the lab; the parts database confirms the bolt's lab origin, and the tool log shows she signed out a 16mm bolt set that very morning and never returned it. Her colleague (w3) says Blessing asked her to lie and left the lab flustered at 1:30, contradicting her alibi. Motive: a written grievance over the lost promotion with a 'make them regret it' threat (r3). The obvious suspects are herrings: the bitter engineer Ifeanyi was at the packaging dock per his card and only talked of striking; the fired cleaner Sunday sent angry threats but gate CCTV proves he never entered (dead-end lookup); and the manager was on a continuous HQ video call. Old gearbox wear-shavings were an unrelated red herring.

title: The Substation Fire
brief: A transformer at the Ikeja North feeder substation explodes at 2:14 a.m., plunging three districts into darkness and costing the DisCo a fortune in a week when a privatisation audit is underway. The blast was no accident: a bypassed protection relay let the fault run unchecked. Someone inside the control room arranged it.

suspects.name: Engr. Tunde Bakare
suspects.motive: Passed over for the regional manager post given to a younger colleague; bitter and vocal about it.
suspects.alibi: Says he was asleep at home in Ogba; lives alone.
suspects.note: Loud, aggrieved, keeps steering talk to his grievance.

suspects.name: Chidinma Okafor
suspects.motive: Recently disciplined for an unauthorised relay test; could be hiding negligence.
suspects.alibi: On the logged night shift in the control room until 1:50 a.m., then signed out.
suspects.note: Precise, defensive about her competence.

suspects.name: Alhaji Musa Danjuma
suspects.motive: His firm's service contract was about to be cancelled by the audit; the fire keeps the DisCo dependent on him.
suspects.alibi: At a wedding in Abeokuta until late; many guests saw him.
suspects.note: Smooth, generous with names of witnesses.

suspects.name: Femi Adeyemi
suspects.motive: Heavily in debt; rumoured to have taken money to 'look away'.
suspects.alibi: Logged as the on-duty operator at the time of the blast.
suspects.note: Nervous, sweats, over-explains.

suspects.name: Grace Eze
suspects.motive: Could bury her own findings of theft by blaming a 'technical failure'.
suspects.alibi: At her hotel in Ikeja; CCTV pending.
suspects.note: Cool, asks more questions than she answers.

findings.heading: Relay bypass
findings.detail: Differential protection relay was placed in 'maintenance/inhibit' mode via the SCADA console, so the relay never tripped the breaker when the fault began.

findings.heading: Inhibit timestamp
findings.detail: The inhibit command was issued at 02:02 — twelve minutes before the blast. Not a standing test; a deliberate, fresh command.

findings.heading: Oil contamination
findings.detail: Insulating oil showed moisture above limits, consistent with overdue maintenance.

findings.heading: No physical tamper
findings.detail: No signs of forced entry to the transformer bay; everything was done from the console.

reports.title: SCADA Console Audit Log
reports.subtitle: Who issued what, and from where

findings.heading: Inhibit command origin
findings.detail: The 02:02 relay-inhibit command came from Console B, authenticated under operator Femi Adeyemi's login, which was active all night.

findings.heading: Shared session warning
findings.detail: Console B logged a 'session unlocked from idle' event at 02:00 with no password re-entry — the workstation had been left logged in and was used by whoever was physically there.

findings.heading: Chidinma's sign-out
findings.detail: Technician Okafor's badge logged an exit at 01:50; the badge does not reappear inside.

findings.heading: Earlier test
findings.detail: A routine relay test was logged at 22:30 and properly restored at 22:41.

findings.heading: Camera blind spot
findings.detail: Camera over Console B failed three days ago and was not repaired; Console A is covered.

findings.heading: Operator at Console A
findings.detail: Femi Adeyemi is on camera at Console A continuously from 01:30 to 02:20, never approaching Console B.

findings.heading: Second person
findings.detail: At 01:58 a figure in a contractor's hi-vis vest enters frame edge and moves toward the (off-camera) Console B; build and gait do not match Femi.

witnesses.name: Sgt. Ibrahim Lawal
witnesses.statement: Only two people badged in after midnight: the night-shift girl who left around ten-to-two, and the operator boy who never came out till the boom. Nobody else came through my gate.

witnesses.name: Blessing Umoh
witnesses.statement: There is a side door by the cable yard that the contractors prop open with a brick so they don't have to walk round. It doesn't badge anybody.

witnesses.name: Femi Adeyemi
witnesses.statement: I never touched the relay. I was at my desk. Somebody used my screen — I leave it open, everybody does, it's against the rules but everybody does it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Where were you at two this morning?

lines.speaker: Bakare
lines.text: Asleep. Alone. That post should have been mine — they gave it to a boy who can't tell a relay from a radio.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Could you inhibit a relay from home?

lines.speaker: Bakare
lines.text: Nobody can. SCADA only takes commands from inside that room. That's the whole point of the room.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who could reach Console B?

lines.speaker: Bakare
lines.text: Anyone standing in front of it, if the operator left it logged on — which Femi always does, the lazy one.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You sound like you know that console well.

lines.speaker: Bakare
lines.text: I supervised that room for nine years. I know everything except how to be at home asleep and in two places at once.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The inhibit command was under your login.

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: Because I never logged out! That's all it proves. I was at Console A the whole time — your own camera shows it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who walked in at 01:58?

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: A maintenance vest. I assumed it was one of Alhaji's boys doing oil checks, they come odd hours. I didn't look up properly.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Alhaji was in Abeokuta.

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: Then it wasn't his boy, was it? But the vest — anybody can wear that vest. They hang in the cable yard. Anybody who's worked here knows where.

timeline.event: Routine relay test completed and restored
Femi seen at Console A (camera)
Chidinma badges out of the building
Hi-vis figure enters, moves to Console B
Console B 'unlocked from idle', no password
Relay inhibit issued from Console B under Femi's login
Transformer T2 arcs and explodes
Bakare's phone connects to a mast near the substation

tools.name: Personnel & Badge Identity
tools.tagline: Match badges to people and credentials

rows.label: Note
rows.value: Privilege not needed if console already authenticated

results.note: Wide access; doesn't single anyone out.

rows.label: Result
rows.value: Vests unnumbered, stored in open rack at cable yard

results.note: Anyone could take one.

tools.name: Mast Triangulation
tools.tagline: Locate a handset by tower hand-offs

results.note: He arrives AFTER the blast — drawn by the alarm, like half the staff. Not before.
She is demonstrably leaving the area during the inhibit window.
Suggestive of a bribe — but the payer was 200km away that night.
Auditor was at her hotel; cleared.

rows.label: Record
rows.value: Grievance complaints only; no sabotage history

results.note: Angry, not criminal.

rows.value: Cadence matches enrolled profile: T. BAKARE

results.note: The person who typed the inhibit had Bakare's stored typing cadence.

explanation: Engr. Tunde Bakare did it. Chain: (r2) the inhibit was issued from Console B, which had been left logged in under Femi's name and was 'unlocked from idle' with no password at 02:00 — so the login proves nothing about identity; whoever was physically standing there did it. (r3) the camera shows Femi never left Console A and a hi-vis figure entered at 01:58 moving to the off-camera Console B — Femi is bodily ruled out. The decisive synthesis is (tool-crime) Console B's keystroke-cadence biometric matching Bakare's enrolled profile for the 02:00–02:03 inhibit, combined with witness w2's propped side door that lets someone enter without badging — explaining how Bakare got in past the gate (Sgt. Lawal only saw the front gate). Bakare wore a vest from the open rack he knew about from nine years supervising the room. Herrings: Femi's ₦400k from Danjuma Services looks like a bribe, but Danjuma (s3) has a wedding-full of witnesses 200km away (alibi confirmed) and the payment was 9 days prior. Femi's login on the inhibit command is the loudest false trail. The 02:18 triangulation putting Bakare 'at the scene' actually clears the timing fear — he's arriving after the blast like other staff — yet his keystroke cadence at 02:02 from inside is the real damning fact. Chidinma (s2) badged out and her phone proves she was a kilometre away and moving during the inhibit window. Grace (s5) was online at her hotel all night. The oil contamination and the earlier 22:41 relay test are pure noise.

title: The Benin Bronze Swap
brief: A 16th-century Benin bronze plaque, loaned to a private museum in Benin City for a homecoming exhibition, is discovered to be a resin forgery the morning after the gala. The real plaque was switched sometime during the event. Five people had the access and the knowledge to do it — and one of them needed the original to settle a very modern debt.

suspects.name: Dr. Osaze Igbinedion
suspects.motive: Custodian of the piece; in quiet financial trouble, his foundation underfunded.
suspects.alibi: Hosting the gala on the floor the entire evening; dozens saw him.
suspects.note: Charming, grief-stricken, helpful to a fault.

suspects.name: Aisha Bello
suspects.motive: Only person with the skill to cast a museum-grade resin copy.
suspects.alibi: In the conservation lab 'doing final checks' alone for part of the night.
suspects.note: Brilliant, terse, resents being the obvious suspect.

suspects.name: Chief Nosa Eweka
suspects.motive: Publicly demanded the bronze stay in Benin forever; a 'repatriation by theft' zealot.
suspects.alibi: At the high table all night, then driven home by his aide.
suspects.note: Booming, righteous, openly hostile to the foreign lender.

suspects.name: Ehi Aigbe
suspects.motive: Cash-strapped; handled the only crate that left the building that night.
suspects.alibi: Loading the deinstall crate at the dock 23:30–00:30.
suspects.note: Eager, defers to everyone, jingling keys.

suspects.name: Linda Okocha
suspects.motive: Could profit from a fraudulent claim, or be covering a prior switch.
suspects.alibi: Arrived only the next morning to inspect; flight records pending.
suspects.note: Clinical, asks for paperwork before answers.

findings.heading: Material
findings.detail: The object on display is pigmented resin with a bronze-powder surface coat. Not metal. A forgery.

findings.heading: Casting age
findings.detail: Resin curing markers indicate the copy was cast 5–8 weeks ago — long before the gala.

findings.heading: Mould source
findings.detail: Surface micro-detail shows the copy was moulded directly off the ORIGINAL, requiring extended unsupervised access to the real plaque weeks earlier.

findings.heading: Tool marks
findings.detail: Edge filing consistent with professional conservation tools.

findings.heading: Gala-night vault
findings.detail: The display vault was opened only twice on gala night: once by the curator for the unveiling, once by security at close. No covert opening.

findings.heading: Off-hours lab access
findings.detail: Six weeks ago, the plaque was signed out to the conservation lab for 'condition reporting' for nine days — far longer than a report needs.

findings.heading: Sign-out authoriser
findings.detail: That nine-day lab loan was authorised on the curator's account but the daily handling log was completed and signed by the conservator each day.

findings.heading: Crate scan
findings.detail: Gala-night deinstall crate was X-rayed at the dock; contents matched the manifest, no plaque-shaped metal object hidden.

findings.heading: Curator's foundation
findings.detail: Underfunded but no large inflows; his debts are old and static.

findings.heading: Anonymous escrow
findings.detail: A London antiquities broker placed $90,000 in escrow 'pending authentication of a West African bronze', released to a Nigerian account ten days ago — BEFORE the gala.

findings.heading: Receiving account
findings.detail: Escrow funds landed in a company, 'Edo Heritage Crafts Ltd', whose sole signatory is masked behind a nominee.

findings.heading: Crating firm cashflow
findings.detail: Ehi Aigbe's firm took a ₦2m short-term loan two weeks ago — for a truck repair, documented.

witnesses.name: Patience Idahosa
witnesses.statement: During those nine days the plaque was in the lab, the restorer kept the side bench under a dust sheet and asked me not to touch it. I thought she was just being careful, the way she is.

witnesses.name: Tony Osagie
witnesses.statement: I shot the plaque close up at the unveiling, around eight. Honestly it already looked a touch too even, too matte. I assumed it was the lighting.

witnesses.name: Chief Nosa Eweka
witnesses.statement: If I could keep that bronze in Benin by any means, God knows I would. But I never left that high table. Ask the governor — he was beside me the whole night.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You had the plaque in your lab for nine days.

lines.speaker: Aisha
lines.text: For a condition report. Yes. Standard.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Nine days is long for a report.

lines.speaker: Aisha
lines.text: There was active corrosion. I documented everything. Check the log — I signed every day.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Could you cast a resin copy from a piece like that?

lines.speaker: Aisha
lines.text: Technically, anyone with a mould could. The skill isn't the casting, it's getting the patina right — and getting it photographed first.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You said 'photographed'.

lines.speaker: Aisha
lines.text: To match the patina. From reference images. That's all I meant. I was describing a process, not confessing to one.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Your crate was the only thing that left the building.

lines.speaker: Ehi
lines.text: And you X-rayed it! Nothing metal, no plaque. I begged you to check, you checked.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The loan you took — two million.

lines.speaker: Ehi
lines.text: For my truck. I have the receipt, the mechanic, everything. I move boxes, oga. I wouldn't know a real bronze from a doorstop.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who told you which crate to load?

lines.speaker: Ehi
lines.text: The deinstall sheet. Madam restorer prepped it and the curator signed it. I just carry what the paper says to carry.

timeline.event: Resin copy cast (per curing markers)
Plaque signed out to lab for 9 days
$90k escrow released to 'Edo Heritage Crafts'
Plaque unveiled (already a forgery per photographer)
Ehi loads deinstall crate (X-rayed clean)
Chief Eweka leaves high table briefly (10 min)
Forgery discovered; insurer arrives

results.note: The escrow money flowed to the conservator, via a shell she set up before the loan even arrived.

rows.label: Result
rows.value: Only the registered foundation; no offshore links

results.note: Curator clear of the receiving account.

rows.label: Uploads
rows.value: 210 macro images of BB-114, all angles

results.note: Reference set needed to fake the patina — exactly the 'photographed first' she described.
Nothing art-related.
Eweka's single call is about his public campaign; Aisha's pattern is transactional.

rows.label: Flight
rows.value: Landed morning after gala; not in city before

results.note: Linda Okocha couldn't have made the swap.

rows.label: Flag
rows.value: Vocal repatriation activist; no theft record

results.note: Loud cause, clean record.

rows.label: Resin batch
rows.value: Same supplier lot as lab stock

results.note: The copy was made with the museum's own lab materials.

explanation: Aisha Bello, the conservator, did it. The whole crime predates the gala — that's the trick. Chain: (r1) the forgery was cast 5–8 weeks ago and moulded directly off the original, requiring extended unsupervised access; (r2) the original was signed out to her lab for an unnecessary nine days six weeks earlier — exactly that access — and the gala-night crate was X-rayed clean, proving nothing physically left that night; the swap had already happened. The decisive money link is (r3) the $90k escrow released BEFORE the gala to 'Edo Heritage Crafts Ltd', which (tool-id) KYC ties to A. Bello via a shell she incorporated 11 weeks ago. Corroborated by tool-phone (210 macro mould-prep images in her cloud during the loan) and tool-crime (copy made from the museum's own resin lot and lab silicone). Herrings, each a stronger surface suspect: Chief Eweka (s3) has the loudest motive ('keep it in Benin') and even leaves the high table for 10 minutes — but the plaque was already fake by unveiling at 20:00, so his absence is irrelevant; his single London call is about his public campaign. Ehi (s4) handled the only crate and took a suspicious loan, but the X-ray clears the crate and his loan is a documented truck repair. The curator (s1) authorised the loan and is in money trouble, but he has no offshore account and never called the broker; his sign-out authority is the misdirection. Linda (s5) only flew in the next morning. The everyone-was-watching-the-gala framing makes people hunt for a gala-night switch that never happened.

title: The Cocoa Ledger
brief: A farmers' cooperative in Ondo State that exports cocoa discovers ₦340 million missing across one season: phantom tonnage, ghost suppliers, and inflated freight. The board demands a culprit before the AGM. Several officers had their hands on the books — but only one engineered the gap, and the cleverest cover was making someone else look guilty.

suspects.name: Bayo Falade
suspects.motive: Controls disbursements; lavish lifestyle the salary can't explain.
suspects.alibi: N/A (financial crime over months).
suspects.note: Flashy, defensive, blames 'the system'.

suspects.name: Funmilayo Adeola
suspects.motive: Posted most of the suspicious entries; in the system daily.
suspects.alibi: N/A.
suspects.note: Meticulous, frightened, keeps printing screenshots.

suspects.name: Engr. Kola Ojo
suspects.motive: Approved the freight contracts; up for re-election, wants no scandal.
suspects.alibi: N/A.
suspects.note: Statesmanlike, deflects to 'due process'.

suspects.name: Ibrahim Sani
suspects.motive: Certifies tonnage; could pass phantom weights.
suspects.alibi: N/A.
suspects.note: Plain-spoken, irritated, points at the office staff.

suspects.name: Grace Mbah
suspects.motive: Could shape blame; paid by the chairman.
suspects.alibi: N/A.
suspects.note: Crisp, careful with wording, never volunteers.

findings.heading: Ghost suppliers
findings.detail: Eleven 'farmer-suppliers' were paid for cocoa with no weighbridge ticket. Bank accounts trace to three numbers.

findings.heading: Inflated freight
findings.detail: Freight invoices padded ~22%; the haulier confirms lower agreed rates.

findings.heading: Entry authorship
findings.detail: All eleven ghost-supplier entries were posted under the chief accountant's user ID, Funmilayo.

findings.heading: Approval override
findings.detail: Each ghost payment cleared a control because it carried a treasurer's 'pre-approved supplier' flag, which only the treasurer's role can set.

findings.heading: After-hours posting
findings.detail: Nine of the eleven ghost entries were posted between 21:00 and 23:00 from inside the office network.

findings.heading: Funmilayo's hours
findings.detail: Funmilayo's badge shows she leaves by 18:00 daily (school-run); she has no after-hours building access.

findings.heading: Shared terminal
findings.detail: The after-hours sessions came from the accounts terminal, where Funmilayo's password was saved/auto-filled — a known office habit.

findings.heading: Password resets
findings.detail: Funmilayo's password was reset by IT twice this season after lockouts.

findings.heading: Ghost account ownership
findings.detail: The three receiving accounts were opened with a BVN that links them all to one identity behind a trading name, 'Falade & Sons Produce'.

findings.heading: Freight kickback
findings.detail: The padded freight overage was split with the haulier; the haulier names the chairman's office as the contact who set rates.

findings.heading: Auditor fee
findings.detail: Grace Mbah's audit fee was standard and paid by cheque; no irregular inflows.

findings.heading: Weighbridge gratuities
findings.detail: Small cash 'dash' to the warehouse officer for fast clearances — petty, customary, ₦tens of thousands.

witnesses.name: Segun Aderibigbe
witnesses.statement: After everybody goes, sometimes Oga Treasurer comes back to 'finish reports'. He uses madam accountant's desk because his own computer is slow, he says. I lock up after him.

witnesses.name: Hauliers' rep (Musa)
witnesses.statement: The rate was set by the chairman's people. We padded and shared, yes. But the farmer payments? That's not us, we only carry the bags that exist.

witnesses.name: Funmilayo Adeola
witnesses.statement: Those entries are under my name but I never made them. I'm gone by six every day for my children. Pull the door logs — I am not in that building at night.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Eleven ghost suppliers cleared a control only your role can set.

lines.speaker: Bayo
lines.text: The 'pre-approved' flag? Anyone could have used my role if they had my login. Funmilayo posted those entries, not me. Her name is on every one.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: We didn't say the flag and the posting were the same person.

lines.speaker: Bayo
lines.text: Well — obviously the same session. You post and you approve, na one workflow.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Were you ever in the office at night this season?

lines.speaker: Bayo
lines.text: Maybe once or twice. My machine is slow. I'd use any free desk to finish. That's not a crime.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Whose desk?

lines.speaker: Bayo
lines.text: Whichever is free. I don't keep a register of chairs, Detective.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The ghost entries are under your user ID.

lines.speaker: Funmi
